items.question_text: Control responsibility within Canadian airspace must be _____ delegated to the FAA by the Transport Canada Aviation Group before the FAA applies its procedures there.
items.explanation: The delegation must be formal — this is the prerequisite condition in 12-1-1. Without formal delegation, the FAA has no control responsibility in Canadian airspace and the chapter's procedural framework does not activate. The word "formally" distinguishes an official transfer from ad hoc or informal arrangements.

choices.text: formally

items.question_text: In Canadian Class B airspace, ATC procedures pertinent to _____ flights must be applied to CVFR aircraft.
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2b, ATC applies IFR procedures to CVFR aircraft. This means CVFR aircraft receive the same separation, sequencing, and control treatment as IFR aircraft — the pilot's visual responsibility to the ground does not reduce ATC's procedural obligations.

choices.text: IFR

items.question_text: In Canadian Class D airspace, VFR flights must establish two-way communications with the appropriate ATC agency _____ to entering the airspace.
items.explanation: The timing requirement is explicit: communications must be established prior to entry, not after penetrating the airspace. This distinguishes Class D from Class C (where a clearance is required) and Class E (where no special VFR requirements exist).

choices.text: prior

items.question_text: What defines Canadian Class F airspace?
items.explanation: Class F is airspace of defined dimensions established for specific activities. It serves two purposes (either or both): confining activities that must be contained because of their nature, and imposing limitations on aircraft not participating in those activities. The sub-types — Class F advisory and Class F restricted — correspond to these purposes. This is a uniquely Canadian airspace class with no U.S. equivalent.

choices.text: Airspace of defined dimensions within which activities must be confined because of their nature, or within which limitations are imposed upon aircraft operations that are not a part of those activities, or both.
Airspace where only military aircraft are permitted to operate without restriction.
Airspace where ATC provides advisory service only, with no authority to issue clearances or instructions to any aircraft.
Temporary airspace activated by NOTAM for air shows, rocket launches, and other special events only.

items.question_text: Which correctly states the flight rules permitted in Canadian Class A airspace versus Canadian Class B airspace?
items.explanation: Class A and Class B represent the most restrictive Canadian controlled airspace, but they differ in one critical way: Class A permits only IFR flights, while Class B permits IFR and CVFR flights. Ordinary VFR is prohibited in both. Confusing these classes could lead a controller to clear VFR traffic into Class A or fail to apply IFR procedures to a CVFR aircraft in Class B.

choices.text: Class A: IFR and CVFR only. Class B: IFR only.
Class A: IFR only. Class B: IFR and CVFR only.
Class A: IFR only. Class B: IFR, CVFR, and VFR.
Class A: IFR and CVFR. Class B: IFR, CVFR, and VFR with two-way communications.

items.question_text: In Canadian airspace, the prescribed phraseology for the substitute clearance used in lieu of VFR-on-top is "at least _____."
items.explanation: The exact prescribed phraseology is "at least 1,000 feet-on-top." This is the Canadian equivalent of the U.S. "VFR-on-top" clearance and must be spoken as written. The phrase communicates both the vertical requirement (1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation) and the pilot's weather responsibility.

choices.text: 1,000 feet-on-top

items.question_text: The Canadian separation rule requires ATC to apply a separation minimum between aircraft on IFR or CVFR clearances. Which aircraft type falls outside this obligation?
items.explanation: The separation obligation in 12-1-4 applies specifically and exclusively to aircraft operating under an IFR or CVFR clearance. An aircraft operating as ordinary VFR — without an IFR or CVFR clearance — is outside the scope of this separation requirement. This is a critical boundary: CVFR aircraft receive IFR-equivalent separation, but ordinary VFR aircraft do not, even in the same airspace volume.

choices.text: An aircraft operating under a CVFR clearance in excellent visual meteorological conditions.
An aircraft on an IFR clearance that has reported the preceding traffic in sight.
An aircraft operating as ordinary VFR without an IFR or CVFR clearance.
A CVFR aircraft that is maintaining continuous visual reference to the ground.

items.question_text: For a turbine-powered aircraft in Canadian airspace that loses communications after departure, the pilot maintains the last assigned altitude or flight level or the MEA, whichever is _____, until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit.
items.explanation: The pilot must maintain the higher of the two altitudes: the last assigned altitude/flight level or the minimum en route IFR altitude (MEA). This ensures terrain and obstruction clearance during the hold period. The "whichever is higher" comparison is a safety floor — the MEA may be higher than the assigned altitude if the assigned altitude was for a terminal procedure or an intermediate step-climb.

choices.text: higher

items.question_text: Under the Canadian radio-failure procedure, a turbine-powered aircraft cleared to a point other than its destination must maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) until _____ minutes beyond the clearance limit before proceeding at filed altitudes.
items.explanation: The hold period is 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit. This 10-minute interval provides time for ATC to recognize the lost-communications condition and clear the route ahead before the aircraft transitions to its filed altitude. The 10-minute value is prescriptive — it is not adjustable by the pilot or controller unless alternate instructions have been issued.

items.question_text: In Canadian airspace, do _____ authorize parachute jumping without prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority.
items.explanation: This is an absolute prohibition: "Do not authorize." The controller has no discretion to approve a parachute jump based on local conditions, pilot assurances, or operational convenience. The prior permission from the appropriate Canadian authority is a non-waivable prerequisite — without it, the answer must always be no.

choices.text: not

items.question_text: When an airport controller on duty approves an SVFR request in a Canadian control zone, the controller must specify the _____ during which SVFR flight is permitted.
items.explanation: The controller must specify the period of time during which SVFR is permitted. This time limitation is a required element of the SVFR approval — it gives ATC control over how long the SVFR operation may continue and allows planning around anticipated IFR traffic. Without a specified period, the approval is incomplete.

choices.text: period of time

items.question_text: A pilot without an IFR qualification wants to fly SVFR at night in a Canadian control zone in an aircraft lacking IFR equipment. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: Canadian SVFR rules explicitly permit night SVFR without IFR pilot qualification and without IFR aircraft equipment. This is a significant difference from U.S. rules under 14 CFR 91.157, which require an IFR-rated pilot and an IFR-equipped aircraft for night SVFR. Controllers working Canadian airspace must not impose U.S. night SVFR requirements on operations governed by Canadian regulations.

choices.text: No — the pilot must hold an IFR rating for any night SVFR operation, consistent with U.S. rules.
No — the aircraft must be IFR-equipped for night SVFR, though the pilot may operate without an IFR rating.
Yes — but only if the pilot has logged at least 50 hours of night flight time in the preceding 12 months.
Yes — Canadian rules permit night SVFR without IFR pilot qualification or IFR aircraft equipment, which differs from U.S. requirements.

items.situation: You are working Canadian airspace where control responsibility has been formally delegated to the FAA. A Canadian-specific procedure in Chapter 12 requires a different clearance phraseology than the standard FAA phraseology for the same operation. A trainee argues that you should use the FAA phraseology because 12-1-1 says to apply basic FAA procedures.
items.question_text: Which phraseology should be used — the Chapter 12 Canadian phraseology or the standard FAA phraseology?

choices.text: Use the Chapter 12 Canadian phraseology, because the chapter's Canadian-specific procedures must be applied where Canadian regulatory or unique operational requirements make them necessary.
Use the FAA phraseology because basic FAA procedures are the default and Canadian procedures are optional.
Use whichever phraseology the pilot requests, because the delegation agreement lets the pilot choose between the two systems.
Use neither — coordinate with the Canadian authority to determine which phraseology applies to this specific operation.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-1, the rule is "apply basic FAA procedures except for the Canadian procedures contained in this chapter." The exception clause means Chapter 12 procedures take precedence over the corresponding basic FAA procedure where they apply. The trainee's argument omits the exception — the Canadian procedures are mandatory where they exist in the chapter, not optional.

items.situation: An aircraft is cruising at 16,000 feet ASL in Canadian controlled low level airspace. The minimum en route IFR altitude for this sector is 11,000 feet. The pilot is operating under an IFR clearance. A trainee asks what airspace class the aircraft is in and what other flight rules category shares this airspace.
items.question_text: What is the correct airspace classification?

choices.text: Class C airspace, which also permits ordinary VFR flights with an ATC clearance.
Class B airspace, which also permits Controlled VFR (CVFR) flights.
Class A airspace, which permits only IFR flights.
Class E airspace, which also permits VFR flights with no special requirements.

items.explanation: At 16,000 feet ASL, the aircraft is above 12,500 feet ASL (and above the 11,000-foot MEA) and below 18,000 feet ASL — this is Canadian Class B airspace per 12-1-2b. Class B permits only IFR and CVFR flights. Class A would be wrong because Class A begins at the base of controlled high level airspace and is IFR-only. Class C, D, and E are lower-altitude airspace classes with different VFR entry rules.

items.situation: A pilot operating below 18,000 feet ASL requests "at least 1,000 feet-on-top." The pilot confirms they can maintain at least 1,000 feet above all cloud with 3 miles flight visibility. However, the filed route includes a segment through Canadian Class B airspace. No alternate radio failure instructions have been issued.

choices.text: Approve it because the pilot has met the weather requirements and requested the clearance — the route through Class B is irrelevant.
Approve it but instruct the pilot to deviate around the Class B airspace while maintaining 1,000 feet-on-top.
Deny it because the pilot will operate within Class B airspace, which is prohibited for the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance.
Deny it because 1,000 feet-on-top is never permitted on filed routes — it is available only for local operations within visual range of the airport.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-3b, the pilot must not operate within Class A or Class B airspace when using the 1,000-feet-on-top clearance. Even though the pilot meets the weather requirements (pilot's responsibility per the note), the airspace restriction is an independent, non-waivable condition. Both conditions — pilot request plus no Class A/B operation — must be satisfied.

items.instruction: What type of separation should you apply?
items.situation: Two aircraft are on IFR clearances in Canadian airspace on the same airway. One is level at FL 200 and the other at FL 210. The lower aircraft requests a climb to FL 220. The airway is a single protected route with no lateral offset available. Longitudinal separation would require the trail aircraft to reduce speed by 40 knots, creating a disruptive traffic flow.
items.question_text: Which type of separation minimum best resolves this situation?

choices.text: Vertical separation — hold the climbing aircraft at FL 200 until the other aircraft is no longer a factor, then approve the climb.
Longitudinal separation — impose the speed reduction on the trail aircraft and accept the disruption to traffic flow.
Lateral separation — offset the climbing aircraft onto a parallel track outside the airway boundary.
Vertical separation — ensure at least 1,000 feet vertical separation exists between the aircraft throughout the climb by coordinating the timing with the aircraft at FL 210.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, the controller may apply a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum. When lateral separation is unavailable (single airway) and longitudinal separation would be unduly disruptive, vertical separation is the appropriate choice. The rule gives the controller discretion to select any of the three types — it does not prioritize one over another. Here, managing the climb to maintain vertical separation throughout is the most efficient solution.

items.situation: Before departure, you issue a turbine-powered aircraft alternate radio failure instructions specifying that if communications are lost, the aircraft is to climb immediately to FL 280 and proceed direct to the destination. After departure, the aircraft loses communications. The standard Canadian radio-failure procedure would have the aircraft maintain its last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) for 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit before climbing to filed altitude. Another controller argues the standard procedure must still be followed because Canadian regulations require it.
items.question_text: Which procedure should the pilot follow?

choices.text: The alternate instructions issued by ATC, because the standard procedure applies only when ATC has NOT issued alternate radio failure instructions.
The standard procedure, because ATC-issued alternate instructions are advisory only and do not override the regulatory requirement.
Neither — the pilot should squawk 7600 and hold at the clearance limit until communications are restored or 30 minutes have elapsed.
The standard procedure for the first 10 minutes, then the alternate instructions thereafter.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the standard radio-failure procedure is conditional: it applies "except when issued alternate radio failure instructions by ATC." Once ATC issues alternate instructions, those instructions entirely replace the standard procedure. The alternate instructions become the controlling plan. The other controller's argument incorrectly treats the standard procedure as mandatory regardless of ATC action.

items.situation: A helicopter pilot requests SVFR at a Canadian control zone where an airport controller is on duty. The reported ground visibility is 3/4 mile. IFR traffic is light, with one arrival expected in approximately 25 minutes. A trainee says the SVFR must be denied because the visibility is below 1 mile.
items.question_text: Should the helicopter's SVFR request be approved or denied?

choices.text: Deny it — the standard SVFR visibility minimum is 1 mile, and 3/4 mile is below that threshold.
Approve it — the 1/2 mile helicopter minimum is met, IFR traffic is light, and with a controller on duty the approval criteria are satisfied.
Deny it — even though helicopters have a lower visibility minimum, the IFR arrival in 25 minutes requires that SVFR be refused.
Approve it but limit the operation to 10 minutes, because helicopter SVFR at reduced visibility cannot exceed that duration.

items.explanation: Two rules combine here. First, the helicopter SVFR visibility minimum is 1/2 mile (12-1-7c2), not the 1 mile that applies to fixed-wing aircraft. At 3/4 mile, the helicopter meets its minimum. Second, with an airport controller on duty (12-1-7a), the approval decision is based on current or anticipated IFR traffic only — not ceiling and visibility. Light IFR traffic supports approval. The controller must specify the period of SVFR authorization.

items.front: What Canadian entity formally delegates control responsibility to the FAA for Canadian airspace?
items.back: The Transport Canada Aviation Group. This formal delegation is the prerequisite for the FAA to apply its procedures in Canadian airspace under Chapter 12.

items.front: In Canadian Class A airspace, what flight rules are aircraft permitted to operate under?
items.back: Only IFR flights are permitted. Class A is controlled high level airspace from the base of all controlled high level airspace up to and including FL 600. No VFR or CVFR operations are allowed.

items.front: How does ATC's treatment of a CVFR aircraft differ from its treatment of an ordinary VFR aircraft in Canadian airspace?
items.back: ATC applies IFR procedures — including lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minima — to CVFR aircraft regardless of weather. Ordinary VFR aircraft do not receive separation services. The CVFR pilot's visual responsibility to the ground does not reduce ATC's separation obligation.

items.front: When a pilot requests "at least 1,000 feet-on-top" in Canadian airspace, what weather-related responsibility does the controller NOT have?
items.back: The controller does not independently verify weather. Weather adequacy is the pilot's responsibility — the pilot's request confirms conditions are adequate (at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation, with 3 miles flight visibility).

items.front: What condition triggers the Canadian radio-failure border-crossing climb rule, and when does the climb commence?
items.back: Trigger: the aircraft will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after passing the clearance limit. Climb timing: the climb to the flight-planned border-crossing altitude commences at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary — NOT 10 minutes after the clearance limit and NOT only after entering U.S. airspace.

items.front: What specific form of authorization do Canadian regulations require before parachute jumping may be conducted?
items.back: Written authority from the Ministry of Transport. Verbal confirmations, NOTAM filings, emails from airport managers, or pilot assurances do not satisfy this requirement. The controller must not authorize a parachute jump without confirming this prior written permission exists.

items.front: What additional element must an airport controller include when approving an SVFR request in a Canadian control zone?
items.back: The period of time during which SVFR flight is permitted. This time specification is a required part of the SVFR approval — it gives ATC control over the operation's duration and enables planning around anticipated IFR traffic.
